structured_choices.data_source: Historical multi-modal sensor readings (audio waveforms, thermal image sequences, and video frames) collected from industrial machinery in a single manufacturing facility over 12 months. Data includes 100,000 labeled maintenance events with ground truth labels assigned by maintenance technicians at the time of intervention. Raw sensor streams are timestamped and aligned to maintenance logs.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary classification: failure (label=1) defined as a maintenance event where technicians diagnosed and repaired a mechanical defect within 48 hours of sensor anomaly detection; normal operation (label=0) defined as sensor readings during documented periods of no failure (verified by maintenance logs showing no repairs performed within a 7-day window). Events lacking clear ground truth or occurring during ambiguous periods are excluded from analysis.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with machine-level stratification. Data is divided chronologically: months 1–8 (training, 67%), months 9–10 (validation, 17%), months 11–12 (test, 17%). Within each split, data is further stratified by equipment ID (machine type and serial number) to ensure no single machine's data dominates any split and to allow assessment of generalization across equipment. This temporal approach prevents information leakage from future failures into training, while machine stratification addresses heterogeneity in failure modes and sensor characteristics across different machines.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (audio MFCC extraction parameters, thermal image normalization statistics, video frame standardization, and any scaling or imputation) are fit exclusively on the training split (months 1–8). The fitted parameters are then applied consistently to validation and test sets. This prevents validation and test performance from being optimistic due to statistics learned from those sets. Synchronization logic (timestamp alignment, frame-rate matching) is designed once on a small labeled sample and applied uniformly to all splits.
structured_choices.features: Audio: Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs) extracted at 100ms windows with 13 coefficients, plus spectral centroid and zero-crossing rate. Thermal: Mean, max, and std of pixel intensities per frame; local hotspot detection (pixels >95th percentile). Visual: Optical flow magnitude (frame-to-frame motion), edge density via Canny detection. All modalities: rolling statistics (mean, max, std) computed over 5-minute windows to capture temporal context. No derived feature encodes the maintenance label or future information; all features are computed from raw sensor streams using domain-standard techniques.
structured_choices.model: A 3-stream CNN architecture: separate convolutional pathways for audio spectrograms, thermal image sequences, and optical flow; concatenated fully connected layers (512 → 256 → 128 units with ReLU and 0.3 dropout); final binary sigmoid output. Total parameters ~2.1M. Model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer (lr=1e-3) with early stopping on validation loss (patience=10 epochs). Class weight balancing is applied: weight_positive = (n_negative / n_positive), fit on training data only.
structured_choices.baseline: Decision tree ensemble (Random Forest, 100 trees, max_depth=15). The baseline receives identical preprocessing and feature engineering as the CNN (same MFCC/thermal/optical flow features, same 5-minute rolling windows, same class weights). The baseline is tuned with the same care: hyperparameters (n_trees, max_depth, min_samples_split) optimized via grid search on the validation set (same validation split as CNN). Both models evaluated on identical test set under identical metrics. This ensures the comparison tests the hypothesis about CNN architecture, not differences in preprocessing or tuning effort.
structured_choices.primary_metric: F1-score (macro-averaged across normal and failure classes). Justification: Stakeholders care about both false positives (unnecessary maintenance, costs, disruption) and false negatives (missed failures, downtime, safety). Macro F1 gives equal weight to both classes despite imbalance, directly aligning with operational cost minimization. F1 penalizes both false alarms and missed failures equally, reflecting the balanced concern stated by stakeholders.
secondary_metrics: Precision: captures false positive rate (unnecessary interventions)
Recall: captures false negative rate (missed failures leading to downtime)
ROC-AUC: threshold-invariant ranking metric, useful if deployment threshold is later tuned
Calibration curve and expected calibration error (ECE): ensures predicted probabilities are reliable for risk-based decision thresholds
Per-machine F1: stratified by equipment ID to detect if either model fails systematically on specific machinery
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 9–10, 17% of data) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. For the CNN: learning rate, dropout rate, and layer width are tuned via random search (50 trials, validation F1 as objective). For Random Forest: n_trees, max_depth, min_samples_split are tuned via grid search (3×3×3=27 configurations, validation F1 as objective). The hyperparameters achieving best validation F1 are locked, and both models are evaluated once on the held-out test set (months 11–12).
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is generated once at the start, clearly isolated, and never used for hyperparameter tuning, feature engineering decisions, or threshold selection. After both models are fully trained and locked, each is run once on the test set to compute all primary and secondary metrics. No retraining, threshold tuning, or metric shopping on test data is permitted. Test results are recorded with 95% confidence intervals (via 1000-sample bootstrap over the test set).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Operational confounds (usage pattern changes, undocumented maintenance, environmental shifts) are mitigated by: (1) temporal stratification within each equipment ID to check for performance drift across the 12-month period (train/val/test results compared for temporal consistency); (2) exclusion criteria for ambiguous labels (events without clear maintenance records or occurring in overlapping windows); (3) per-machine stratification in the split to measure if CNN advantage holds uniformly across different machines or is concentrated in specific equipment; (4) post-hoc analysis of failure mode distribution (mechanical vs. electrical, seal vs. bearing) across splits to detect if any failure type is over-represented in validation/test. If significant confounding is detected (e.g., specific machines appear only in test), the comparison is re-run with stratification by failure type or machine ID as a sensitivity check.

design_narrative:
To test whether a CNN on multi-modal sensor data outperforms decision trees in early mechanical failure detection, we propose the following methodologically sound design:

**1. Data and Labeling**

We source 12 months of timestamped multi-modal sensor streams (audio waveforms, thermal image sequences, video frames) from a single manufacturing facility, covering approximately 100,000 maintenance events across multiple machines. Labels are assigned by cross-referencing sensor timestamps with maintenance logs: a failure (label=1) is a maintenance event where technicians documented and repaired a mechanical defect within 48 hours of the sensor anomaly; normal operation (label=0) is any 7-day window with no repairs logged. Events with ambiguous ground truth (e.g., overlapping repair windows, missing logs) are excluded to ensure label integrity.

**2. Temporal and Stratified Split**

Data is divided chronologically to prevent information leakage: months 1–8 form the training set (67%), months 9–10 the validation set (17%), and months 11–12 the held-out test set (17%). Within each time window, we further stratify by machine ID (serial number and type) to ensure each equipment model appears proportionally in all splits and to enable per-machine evaluation. This temporal-plus-machine stratification controls for confounds like seasonal operational changes or undocumented maintenance patterns that could bias results toward one model.

**3. Synchronized Multi-Modal Features**

All three modalities are preprocessed with care:
- **Audio**: MFCC features (13 coefficients) computed at 100ms windows, plus spectral centroid and zero-crossing rate.
- **Thermal**: Per-frame mean, max, and standard deviation of pixel intensities; local hotspot detection (pixels exceeding the 95th percentile).
- **Visual**: Optical flow magnitude and edge density (Canny detector) to capture motion and structural changes.

All signals are aligned to a common 5-minute rolling window, computing mean, max, and standard deviation across each modality. Preprocessing transformers (normalization statistics, MFCC parameters) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. No feature is derived from the maintenance label or future data.

**4. Model Architectures and Baseline**

The proposed model is a 3-stream CNN: separate convolutional pathways for audio spectrograms, thermal sequences, and optical flow, with concatenated fully connected layers (512 → 256 → 128 units, ReLU activation, 0.3 dropout) and a final sigmoid output for binary classification. Class weights are applied during training: weight = n_negative / n_positive, fit on training data only.

The baseline is a Random Forest with 100 trees, max_depth=15, trained on the identical feature set and class weights. Both models receive equal tuning effort: the CNN is optimized via random search (50 trials) over learning rate and dropout; the Random Forest via grid search (27 configurations) over tree count and depth. Both use the validation set for model selection, with early stopping on validation F1-score.

**5. Primary and Secondary Metrics**

Primary metric: **F1-score (macro-averaged)**. This balances precision (false positives = unnecessary maintenance disruption) and recall (false negatives = missed failures and downtime), directly reflecting stakeholder concerns about cost and operational continuity. We compute F1 with equal weighting to both classes despite the imbalance, ensuring that the metric penalizes missed failures as heavily as false alarms.

Secondary metrics include per-class precision and recall, ROC-AUC for threshold-sensitivity analysis, calibration curve and expected calibration error (to verify predicted probabilities are reliable for downstream risk decisions), and per-machine F1 (to detect whether either model generalizes uniformly across equipment types).

**6. Validation and Test Protocol**

The validation set (months 9–10) is used only for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Once optimal hyperparameters are locked, both models are trained once on the full training set and evaluated on the held-out test set (months 11–12) a single time. Test performance is reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. No threshold tuning, feature re-engineering, or metric exploration is permitted on the test set.

**7. Confound Controls and Scope**

Operational confounds (usage pattern drift, unlogged maintenance, environmental shifts) are controlled via temporal stratification within machine IDs and post-hoc analysis of failure mode distribution across splits. If significant imbalance is detected (e.g., a specific failure type or machine appears only in the test set), sensitivity analyses rerun the comparison with stratification by failure mode.

This design establishes whether CNN architecture and multi-modal integration offer genuine advantages over decision trees *on this facility's data during this 12-month period*. It does not establish generalization to other facilities, equipment, or time horizons without separate validation.
